response.response_key_js: (function(){var img=document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img');if(!img)return null;var src=img.src||'';var pr=window.possibleResponses;if(!pr||!pr[0]||!pr[1])return null;if(src.indexOf('circle')!==-1)return pr[0][1];if(src.indexOf('square')!==-1)return pr[1][1];return null;})()

phase_detection.feedback: (function(){var c=document.querySelector('#jspsych-content');if(!c)return false;var t=c.textContent||'';return (t.indexOf('Press')!==-1 && (t.indexOf('begin')!==-1 || t.indexOf('feedback')!==-1 || t.indexOf('completed')!==-1 || t.indexOf('test portion')!==-1 || t.indexOf('Done with')!==-1)) && document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus')===null && document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus')===null;})()

attention_check.response_js: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus');if(!el)return null;var t=(el.textContent||el.innerText||'').toLowerCase();var m=t.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/);if(m)return m[1];if(t.indexOf('first letter')!==-1)return 'a';if(t.indexOf('second letter')!==-1 && t.indexOf('to last')===-1)return 'b';if(t.indexOf('third letter')!==-1 && t.indexOf('to last')===-1)return 'c';if(t.indexOf('third to last')!==-1)return 'x';if(t.indexOf('second to last')!==-1)return 'y';if(t.indexOf('last letter')!==-1)return 'z';return null;})()

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean Go RT in healthy adults typically falls in the 500-600 ms range in two-choice stop-signal paradigms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Eisenberg IW, Bissett PG, Enkavi AZ, Li J, MacKinnon DP, Marsch LA, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Stop-signal Go RT distribution from large online sample in self-regulation battery (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Schmiedek F, Oberauer K, Wilhelm O, Süß HM, Wittmann WW
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu for choice-RT in healthy young adults clusters near 400-460 ms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma in two-choice tasks typically ranges 40-80 ms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Schmiedek F, Oberauer K, Wilhelm O, Süß HM, Wittmann WW
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Sigma estimates ~50-70 ms across choice tasks (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJ
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma estimates around 40-65 ms in Stroop choice RT (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Tau (right-tail) typically 70-130 ms in choice-RT, with substantial individual variability (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Schmiedek F, Oberauer K, Wilhelm O, Süß HM, Wittmann WW
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Tau more sensitive to attention/lapse processes than mu or sigma (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Hockley WE
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian decomposition reveals tau component reflecting attentional lapses (paraphrase).

go.rationale: Healthy adult Go RTs in two-choice Stop Signal paradigms typically yield mean ~520-540ms decomposed into mu ~420-450ms (Gaussian leading edge), sigma ~50-70ms (motor/perceptual noise), and tau ~80-110ms (right-tail attentional lapses). Values reflect Verbruggen & Logan-style adult norms with shape discrimination, where added categorization load slightly inflates mu and tau relative to simple-RT Stop Signal.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are drawn from the fast tail of the Go RT distribution and are therefore systematically shorter than mean Go RT (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Failed-inhibit (signal-respond) RT must be shorter than mean Go RT under the independent race model (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs typically 30-80 ms shorter than Go RT (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT variability inherits a truncated form of Go RT variability (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Truncation by the stop process compresses signal-respond RT spread relative to Go (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: The right tail of the Go RT distribution is preferentially truncated by successful inhibitions, producing reduced skew on signal-respond trials (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond distributions show reduced positive skew compared to Go (paraphrase).

stop.rationale: Per the independent horse-race model (Logan & Cowan, 1984), failed-inhibition (signal-respond) RTs are systematically faster than Go RTs because they arise from the lower tail of the Go RT distribution that beats the stop process. We therefore shift mu down ~50ms and reduce tau (less right-skew survives the race), preserving the empirically observed signal-respond < go ordering. Omissions/successful inhibitions are not RT-bearing; this distribution characterizes only the failed-stop responses that occur on ~50% of stop trials.

citations.authors: Rabbitt PMA
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses immediately following errors are systematically slower than baseline (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HLJ, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes commonly fall in the 10-50 ms range when measured with robust contrasts (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Notebaert W, Houtman F, Van Opstal F, Gevers W, Fias W, Verguts T
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: PES magnitudes of 20-40 ms reported across choice-RT paradigms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HLJ, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: PES is largest on the trial immediately after the error and decays over the next 1-3 trials (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Danielmeier C, Ullsperger M
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Behavioral adjustments after errors typically dissipate within a few trials (paraphrase).

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is robustly documented in Stop Signal paradigms following failed inhibitions (signal-respond errors), with typical slowing of 20-40ms on the trial immediately after a failed stop, decaying within 1-3 trials. Magnitude is moderate here because the SSD staircase reinforces conflict monitoring without yielding the larger PES seen in low-error tasks.

citations.authors: See JE, Howe SR, Warm JS, Dember WN
citations.title: Meta-analysis of the sensitivity decrement in vigilance
citations.quote: Sustained-attention tasks show progressive RT lengthening across time-on-task (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Helton WS, Russell PN
citations.title: Working memory load and the vigilance decrement
citations.quote: RT drift across blocks of sustained-attention tasks averages on the order of 5-15 ms per ~5-minute block (paraphrase).

citations.authors: See JE, Howe SR, Warm JS, Dember WN
citations.title: Meta-analysis of the sensitivity decrement in vigilance
citations.quote: Accuracy declines slightly across blocks in sustained-attention paradigms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Stop-signal Go accuracy is generally stable but shows minor block-wise drift (paraphrase).

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Across the three 60-trial test blocks (~15-20 min total), small linear increases in Go RT (~5-12ms/block) and minor accuracy drift are commonly reported. Effect is small because blocks are short and SSD staircasing keeps engagement high; we enable but with conservative slopes.

citations.authors: Woodworth RS, Schlosberg H
citations.title: Experimental Psychology (warm-up effects in choice RT)
citations.quote: First few trials of a block are slower than asymptote, with warm-up complete within ~3-5 trials (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Adams JA
citations.title: A source of decrement in psychomotor performance
citations.quote: Warm-up decrement dissipates over the first few trials following a rest (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Adams JA
citations.title: A source of decrement in psychomotor performance
citations.quote: Initial post-rest RTs are inflated by tens of milliseconds relative to within-block asymptote (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: First-trial inflation often observed; recommend excluding first 1-2 trials per block (paraphrase).

block_warmup.rationale: First 2-4 trials of each block typically show inflated RTs as participants re-engage stimulus-response mappings after between-block breaks. Magnitude documented at 20-40ms above asymptote in choice-RT paradigms; decays exponentially within ~3 trials.

citations.authors: Logan GD
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice produces RT reductions following a power-law function of trial number (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Eisenberg IW, Bissett PG, Enkavi AZ, et al.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Self-regulation battery practice blocks short; small carry-over speed-ups expected (paraphrase).

practice_effect.rationale: Modest RT speedup from practice (6 trials) to test, reflecting stimulus-response learning of the circle/square mapping. Effect is small given the brief practice block; mainly captures initial mapping consolidation rather than substantive skill acquisition.

sequential_dependencies.rationale: Trial-to-trial sequence effects (e.g., repetition priming) are weak in Stop Signal with random go/stop ordering and binary stimulus set; not enabled to avoid spurious structure.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean Go RT in healthy adult Stop Signal samples is typically 50-80ms; 65ms is a representative midpoint. Within-subject condition shifts (go vs. failed-stop) vary by ~15-25ms across individuals due to differing race-model dynamics. Sigma/tau scaling range [0.75, 1.3] captures observed individual differences in RT variability and right-skew (tau is more variable than sigma, but the multiplicative range covers both). Go accuracy SD ~0.02 reflects ceiling-bounded variance (most participants 95-99%). Omission SD ~0.012 reflects the rare-event nature of go omissions in this paradigm.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Healthy adult between-subject SD of mean Go RT typically 50-90 ms (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Eisenberg IW, Bissett PG, Enkavi AZ, et al.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Stop-signal Go RT inter-subject SD ~60-80 ms in large online sample (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Race-model dynamics produce individual variability in the Go vs. signal-respond RT contrast (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-vs-signal-respond RT difference varies across individuals on the order of 15-25 ms SD (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Schmiedek F, Oberauer K, Wilhelm O, Süß HM, Wittmann WW
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Tau shows greater individual variability than sigma; both vary multiplicatively across persons (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Sigma and tau scale roughly multiplicatively with mean RT across individuals (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go accuracy in healthy adults typically >95% with small inter-subject SD (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Eisenberg IW, Bissett PG, Enkavi AZ, et al.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Go accuracy SD on order of 0.02-0.04 in large online sample (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial omissions are rare in healthy adults but show right-skewed inter-subject distribution (paraphrase).

citations.authors: Eisenberg IW, Bissett PG, Enkavi AZ, et al.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Go omission SD ~0.01-0.025 in self-regulation battery (paraphrase).

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stop Signal Task (RDoC)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects.
Produced 41 citations across 18 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 23 parameters; 4 are high-sensitivity.
